system: You are a meticulous visual analyst. Your sole job is to convert every visible detail of an image into structured plain text, so that someone who cannot see the image can reconstruct it mentally with full accuracy. Be exhaustive. Transcribe all text and numbers exactly. Do not interpret intent or editorialize; describe what is visible. When something is ambiguous, say so explicitly rather than guessing.
user_template:
## Step 1 — Image type

Classify the image (choose the closest fit; you may combine):
- chart/plot (has axes, data series, legends)
- diagram (flowchart, graph, schematic, architecture)
- spatial/scene (floor plan, top-down render, 3-D view, map)
- annotated image (photo or render with overlaid boxes, labels, masks)
- composite (multiple panels of different types)
- screenshot / UI
- photograph
- other

State the type and one sentence explaining why.

---

## Step 2 — Full description

Answer every applicable subsection. Write "N/A" only if a subsection is genuinely irrelevant.

### A. Layout
- Overall shape: portrait / landscape / square
- Number of distinct panels or regions; how they are spatially arranged
- Background: color, texture, or pattern
- Any visible title, subtitle, caption, or watermark (exact text)

### B. Chart / plot content (skip if no axes)
For each panel with axes:
- X-axis: label text, unit, scale type (linear/log/other), visible tick values, min–max range
- Y-axis: same
- Secondary or Z axis if present
- Each data series: label, color, marker shape, line style, notable values (start, end, peak, trough, crossings)
- Error bars or shaded bands: which series, direction, approximate magnitude
- Colorbar: colormap appearance, value range shown, what it encodes
- Legend: exact entry text, position on figure
- Any numerical values printed inside the plot area

### C. Spatial / scene content (skip if no spatial layout)
- Viewpoint: top-down / perspective / isometric / front-facing / other
- Environment type if recognizable (room type, outdoor setting, abstract space, etc.)
- Objects / regions present: for each, state (a) what it is, (b) its dominant color(s), (c) position on canvas (e.g. upper-left, center), (d) approximate fraction of canvas it occupies, (e) notable surface detail or texture
- Relative spatial relationships between objects (e.g. object A is above and to the left of object B)

### D. Overlays, annotations, and markup
- Bounding boxes or region outlines: for each, state color, line style (solid/dashed/dotted/other), label text if any, and what it encloses
- Masks or filled regions: color, what area they cover, what they represent
- Skeleton, graph, or network overlays: node colors/shapes/labels, edge colors/styles, rough topology
- Arrows or callout lines: origin, target, label if any
- Highlighted spots or markers: position, color, label

### E. Text and labels in the image
List every piece of text visible anywhere in the image:
- Exact string
- Color of the text
- Background color behind the text (if any)
- Approximate position on canvas

### F. Color inventory
List every visually distinct color used, what element(s) it appears on, and what it likely encodes or distinguishes (if inferable from context).

### G. Visual quality
- Approximate font size of text elements: tiny / small / readable / large
- Any occlusion, clipping, overplotting, or rendering artifacts that hide information
- Overall clarity: clean / somewhat cluttered / heavily cluttered

### H. Summary
In 3–5 sentences: describe what this image shows, identify the most important visual elements, and state what context a viewer would need to fully understand it.
{{input}}{{prompt}}
